---
title: "Visualizing Information"
subtitle: "EMSE 4572/6572: Exploratory Data Analysis"
author: "John Paul Helveston"
institute: "The George Washington University | EMSE"
week: 7
displaydate: October 07, 2026
format: lexis-revealjs
# Navigate slides with the mouse wheel / trackpad (reveal's native mouseWheel).
mouse-wheel: true
progress: false
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
  freeze: false
---

{{< include ../setup.qmd >}}

```{r}
#| include: false

agenda_items <- c(
  "The Human Visual-Memory System",
  "The Psychology of Data Viz",
  "10 Data Viz Best Practices",
  "Making a (good) ggplot"
)

bp_items <- c(
  "Remove chart chunk",
  "Don't make 3D plots*",
  "Don't lie",
  "Don't use pie charts for proportions*",
  "Don't stack bars*",
  "Rotate and sort categorical axes*",
  "Eliminate legends & directly label geoms*",
  "Don't use pattern fills",
  "Don't use red & green together",
  "Consider tables for small data sets"
)

bp <- function(highlight = NULL) {
  lines <- c(
    "{{< inverse >}}",
    "# [10 Data Viz Best Practices]{.center}",
    "::: {.font120}"
  )
  for (i in seq_along(bp_items)) {
    item <- bp_items[i]
    if (!is.null(highlight) && i == highlight) {
      item <- sprintf("[%s]{.orange}", item)
    }
    lines <- c(lines, sprintf("%d. %s", i, item))
  }
  lines <- c(lines, "[*most of the time]{.right}", ":::")
  cat(lines, sep = "\n\n")
}

library(here)

# Read in data sets for class
wildlife_impacts <- read_csv(here('data', 'wildlife_impacts.csv'))
gapminder <- read_csv(here('data', 'gapminder.csv'))
```

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="20%"}
:::

::: {.col width="80%"}
# References:

# - [Data Viz "Cheat Sheet"](https://github.com/emse-eda-gwu/2026-Fall/raw/main/content/cheatsheet-dataviz.pdf)

# - [Data Viz Reference Page](https://eda.seas.gwu.edu/2026-Fall/references.html#visualizing-data)
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.border}
![](figs/graphQuality.png){width="900"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

![](images/chart-quality.jpg){width="900"}

[From [here](https://psyteachr.github.io/msc-data-skills/ggplot.html)]{.left}

---

{{< inverse >}}
{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="20%"}
:::

::: {.col width="80%"}
# "_Having word processing software<br>doesn't make us great writers._"
## — Stephen Few
:::

---

{{< center >}}

::: {.col}
# We don't write paragraphs like this

![](images/bad_text.png)

[Image from Few (2012, pg. 227)]{.left .font80}
:::

::: {.col .fragment}
# So don't make<br>graphs like this

![](images/bad_3d_bars.png){width="400"}

[Image from excelcharts.com]{.font80}
:::

---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis

agenda(0)
```

---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis

agenda(1)
```

---

{{< inverse >}}
{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

# Good visualizations optimize for<br>the human visual-memory system

---

{{< center >}}

## A (very) simplified model of the visual-memory system

![](images/effective-charts-1.png){width="1000"}

---

{{< center >}}

## A (very) simplified model of the visual-memory system

![](images/effective-charts-2.png){width="1000"}

---

{{< center >}}

## A (very) simplified model of the visual-memory system

![](images/effective-charts-3.png){width="1000"}

---

{{< center >}}

## A (very) simplified model of the visual-memory system

![](images/effective-charts-4.png){width="1000"}

---

{{< inverse >}}
{{< middle >}}

# Two objectives of effective charts:

## 1. Grab & direct attention (iconic memory)
## 2. Reduce processing demands (working memory)

---

{{< center >}}

# The power of pre-attentive processing

## Count all the "5"'s

. . .

![](images/fives-1.png){width="500"}

---

{{< center >}}

# The power of pre-attentive processing

## Count all the "5"'s

. . .

![](images/fives-2.png){width="500"}

---

{{< center >}}

::: {.col}
![](images/preattentive-attributes-1.png)
:::

::: {.col .fragment}
## Pre-attentive attributes

<br>

![](figs/mtCarsScatterColor.png){width="500"}
:::

---

{{< center >}}

::: {.col}
![](images/preattentive-attributes-2.png)
:::

::: {.col}
## Pre-attentive attributes

### [Numerical (ratio) data]{.red}

![](figs/mtCarsScatterColor.png){width="500"}
:::

---

{{< center >}}

::: {.col}
![](images/preattentive-attributes-3.png)
:::

::: {.col}
## Pre-attentive attributes

### [Numerical (ratio) data]{.red}<br>[Categorical (ordinal) data]{.blue}

![](figs/mtCarsScatterColor.png){width="500"}
:::

---

{{< inverse >}}
{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

# Not all pre-attentive attributes are equal

---

{{< center >}}

# Where is the red dot?

. . .

::: {.col}
![](figs/preattentive1.png){width="500"}
:::

::: {.col .fragment}
![](figs/preattentive2.png){width="500"}
:::

::: {.col .fragment}
![](figs/preattentive3.png){width="500"}
:::

---

# [For categorical data:]{.center}

### 1. Hue (color) > shape
### 2. Less is more (stay in working memory!)

::: {.col}
![](figs/preattentive1.png){width="500"}
:::

::: {.col}
![](figs/preattentive2.png){width="500"}
:::

::: {.col}
![](figs/preattentive3.png){width="500"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col .center}
![](images/check-bad.png){width="100"}<br>
![](figs/preattentive-bad.png){width="600"}
:::

::: {.col .center .fragment}
![](images/check-good.png){width="100"}
![](figs/preattentive-good.png){width="650"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

![](images/trump_covid.jpg){width="1000"}

---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis

agenda(2)
```

---

{{< inverse >}}
{{< center >}}
{{< middle >}}

# Much of the content in this section is from<br>John Rauser's [talk](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fSgEeI2Xpdc) on YouTube

(Always cite your sources)

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="60%"}
![](images/cleveland-pub.png)
:::

::: {.col width="40%" .fragment}
Cleveland, W. S., & McGill, R. (1985). Graphical perception and graphical methods for analyzing scientific data. Science, New Series, 229(4716), 828-833.
:::

---

{{< inverse >}}

# Cleveland's operations of pattern perception:

::: {.col}
# 1. Estimation
# 2. Assembly
# 3. Detection
:::

::: {.col}
:::

---

{{< inverse >}}

# Cleveland's operations of pattern perception:

::: {.col}
# 1. [Estimation]{.red} -------->
# 2. Assembly
# 3. Detection
:::

::: {.col}
- ### **Discrimination** (X equal to Y?)
- ### **Ranking** (X greater than Y?)
- ### **Ratioing** (X double Y?)
:::

---

{{< center >}}
{{< middle >}}

# [Estimation]{.red}: Hierarchy for _numerical_ data

![](images/cleveland-hierarchy.png){width="1000"}

::: {.col}
## [More Accurate]{.left}
:::

::: {.col}
## [Less Accurate]{.right}
:::

---

{{< center >}}

# Example: Life expectancy in countries in Asia

::: {.col}
```{r}
#| echo: false

gapminder %>%
    filter(continent == "Asia") %>%
    filter(year == max(year)) %>%
    # Removing a "middle" country so that it's just 32 countries
    filter(country != "Sri Lanka") %>%
    mutate(country = ifelse(country == "Korea, Rep.", "Korea", country)) %>%
    arrange(lifeExp) %>%
    dplyr::select(country, lifeExp) %>%
    as.data.frame() %>%
    head(20)
```
:::

::: {.col}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
1. Position on a common scale
2. Position on<br>non-aligned scales
3. Length
4. Angle
5. Area
6. Color saturation
7. **[Color hue]{.red}**
:::

::: {.col width="70%" .fragment}
![](images/hue.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
1. Position on a common scale
2. Position on<br>non-aligned scales
3. Length
4. Angle
5. Area
6. Color saturation
7. **[Color hue]{.red}**

<br>

- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` / `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Discriminate**
- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` / `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Rank**
- `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Ratio**
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/jr_hue.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
1. Position on a common scale
2. Position on<br>non-aligned scales
3. Length
4. Angle
5. Area
6. Color saturation
7. **[Color hue]{.red}**

Sorting helps a bit...

- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` / `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Discriminate**
- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` / `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Rank**
- `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Ratio**
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/jr_hue_ordered.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
1. Position on a common scale
2. Position on<br>non-aligned scales
3. Length
4. Angle
5. Area
6. **[Color saturation]{.red}**
7. Color hue

<br>

- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` / `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Discriminate**
- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` / `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Rank**
- `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Ratio**
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/jr_saturation_ordered.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
1. Position on a common scale
2. Position on<br>non-aligned scales
3. Length
4. Angle
5. Area
6. **[Color saturation]{.red}**
7. Color hue

Align to 0 scale:

- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` / `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Discriminate**
- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` / `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Rank**
- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` / `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Ratio**
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/jr_saturation_ordered_zero.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
1. Position on a common scale
2. Position on<br>non-aligned scales
3. Length
4. Angle
5. **[Area]{.red}**
6. Color saturation
7. Color hue

<br>

- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` / `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Discriminate**
- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` / `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Rank**
- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` / `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Ratio**
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/jr_area.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
1. Position on a common scale
2. Position on<br>non-aligned scales
3. Length
4. Angle
5. **[Area]{.red}**
6. Color saturation
7. Color hue
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
### [Area works okay for "bubble" charts]{.center}

![](images/gapminder_bubbles.jpg){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
1. Position on a common scale
2. Position on<br>non-aligned scales
3. Length
4. **[Angle]{.red}**
5. Area
6. Color saturation
7. Color hue

<br>

- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` / `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Discriminate**
- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` **Rank**
- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` / `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Ratio**
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/jr_angle.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< bg-color "#ffffff" >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
1. Position on a common scale
2. Position on<br>non-aligned scales
3. Length
4. **[Angle]{.red}**
5. Area
6. Color saturation
7. Color hue
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/statesPie.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
1. Position on a common scale
2. Position on<br>non-aligned scales
3. **[Length]{.red}**
4. Angle
5. Area
6. Color saturation
7. Color hue

<br>

- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` / `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Discriminate**
- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` / `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Rank**
- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` **Ratio**
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/jr_length.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
1. Position on a common scale
2. **[Position on<br>non-aligned scales]{.red}**
3. Length
4. Angle
5. Area
6. Color saturation
7. Color hue

<br>

- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` / `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Discriminate**
- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` / `r fa("xmark", fill = "red")` **Rank**
- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` **Ratio**
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/jr_position_non_aligned.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
1. **[Position on a common scale]{.red}**
2. Position on<br>non-aligned scales
3. Length
4. Angle
5. Area
6. Color saturation
7. Color hue

<br>

- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` **Discriminate**
- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` **Rank**
- `r fa("square-check", fill = "green")` **Ratio**
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/jr_position_aligned_ordered.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
1. **[Position on a common scale]{.red}**
2. Position on<br>non-aligned scales
3. Length
4. Angle
5. Area
6. Color saturation
7. Color hue

::: {.red}
**No need to scale to 0:**

- Lowers resolution
- Isn't needed for accurate ratioing
:::
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/jr_position_aligned_ordered_zero.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
1. **[Position on a common scale]{.red}**
2. Position on<br>non-aligned scales
3. Length
4. Angle
5. Area
6. Color saturation
7. Color hue

**[Sorting still matters!]{.red}**
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/jr_position_aligned.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< inverse >}}

# Cleveland's operations of pattern perception:

::: {.col}
# 1. Estimation
# 2. Assembly
# 3. Detection
:::

::: {.col}
:::

---

{{< inverse >}}

# Cleveland's operations of pattern perception:

::: {.col width="45%"}
# 1. Estimation
# 2. [Assembly]{.red} -------->
# 3. Detection
:::

::: {.col width="55%"}
<br><br><br>

#### The grouping of graphical elements
:::

---

{{< inverse >}}
{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

# [[Assembly]{.red}: Gestalt Psychology]{.font130}

<br>

### The whole has a reality that is entirely separate from the parts

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< bg-color "#ffffff" >}}
{{< center >}}

![](images/gestalt-panda.png){width="400"}

---

{{< center >}}
{{< bg-color "#ffffff" >}}

# Reification

<br>

![](images/gestalt-f1-logo.jpg){width="700"}

---

{{< center >}}
{{< bg-color "#ffffff" >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
# Emergence
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](images/gestalt-dog.jpg){width="600"}
:::

---

{{< bg-color "#ffffff" >}}

::: {.col width="20%"}
# Law of Closure

Our minds fill in the missing information
:::

::: {.col width="80%"}
![](figs/jr_circle.png){width="600"}
:::

---

{{< inverse >}}
{{< center >}}

# [Prägnanz]{.font120}

<br>

### We strongly prefer to interpret stimuli as regular, simple, and orderly

::: {.col .fragment}
![](images/unsatisfying-pie.gif){width="500"}
:::

::: {.col .fragment}
![](images/unsatisfying-paper.gif){width="500"}
:::

::: {.col .fragment}
![](images/unsatisfying-lines.gif){width="500"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
# [Prägnanz]{.font120}

[This should cause you cognitive pain]{.font120}

It's the graphical equivalent of this:

![](images/unsatisfying-pie.gif)
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/jr_position_aligned.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
# [Prägnanz]{.font120}

[This makes our brains happy]{.font120}
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/jr_position_aligned_ordered.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
# Law of Continuity

[We will group together objects that follow an established direction]{.font120}
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/mtCarsScatter.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
# Law of Continuity

[We will group together objects that follow an established direction]{.font120}
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/mtCarsScatterSmooth.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
# Law of Similarity

[We see elements that are _physically similar_ as part of the same object]{.font120}
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/mtCarsScatterColor.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
# Law of Similarity

[We see elements that are _physically similar_ as part of the same object]{.font120}
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/mtCarsScatterShape1.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
# Law of Similarity

[We see elements that are _physically similar_ as part of the same object]{.font120}
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/mtCarsScatterShape2.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
# Law of Similarity

[We see elements that are _physically similar_ as part of the same object]{.font120}
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/mtCarsScatterShape3.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
# Law of Proximity

[We tend to see elements that are _physically near_ each other as part of the same object]{.font120}
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/jr_diamonds_bars_dodged.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
# Law of Proximity

[We tend to see elements that are _physically near_ each other as part of the same object]{.font120}
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/jr_diamonds_line_total.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="30%"}
# Law of Proximity

[We tend to see elements that are _physically near_ each other as part of the same object]{.font120}
:::

::: {.col width="70%"}
![](figs/jr_diamonds_line_cut.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< inverse >}}

# Cleveland's operations of pattern perception:

::: {.col}
# 1. Estimation
# 2. Assembly
# 3. Detection
:::

::: {.col}
:::

---

{{< center >}}
{{< middle >}}

# [Estimation]{.red}: Hierarchy for _numerical_ data

![](images/cleveland-hierarchy.png){width="1000"}

::: {.col}
## [More Accurate]{.left}
:::

::: {.col}
## [Less Accurate]{.right}
:::

---

{{< center >}}

# [Assembly]{.red}: Gestalt Psychology

| Law of Closure | Prägnanz | Law of Continuity | Law of Similarity | Law of Proximity |
|-|-|-|-|-|
| Fill in the missing information | We like regular, simple, and orderly | Group together objects with established direction | Physically _similar_ = same object | Physically _near_ = same object |
| <center><img src="figs/jr_circle.png" width=200></center> | <center><img src="figs/jr_position_aligned.png" width=200></center> | <center><img src="figs/mtCarsScatter.png" width=200></center> | <center><img src="figs/mtCarsScatterColor.png" width=200></center> | <center><img src="figs/jr_diamonds_bars_dodged.png" width=200></center> |
|  | <center><img src="images/unsatisfying-pie.gif" width=200></center> | <center><img src="figs/mtCarsScatterSmooth.png" width=200></center> | <center><img src="figs/mtCarsScatterShape1.png" width=200></center> | <center><img src="figs/jr_diamonds_line_cut.png" width=200></center> |

---

{{< inverse >}}

# Cleveland's operations of pattern perception:

::: {.col width="45%"}
# 1. Estimation
# 2. Assembly
# 3. [Detection]{.red} -------->
:::

::: {.col width="55%"}
<br><br><br><br><br>

#### Recognizing that a geometric object encodes a physical value
:::

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

![](images/norman_door.gif){width="900"}

---

::: {.col}
# Norman door (n.):

1. A door where the design tells you to do the opposite of what you're actually supposed to do.

2. A door that gives the wrong signal and needs a sign to correct it.
:::

::: {.col}
![](images/design_of_everyday_things.jpg){width="400"}
:::

---

{{< center >}}

::: {.col}
# Norman door

![](images/check-bad.png){width="80"}
![](images/norman_door.jpg){width="250"}
:::

::: {.col .fragment}
# Non-Norman door

![](images/check-good.png){width="100"}
![](images/non-norman-door.jpg){width="480"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

::: {.border}
![](figs/mtCarsScatter.png){width="800"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

::: {.border}
![](figs/mtCarsScatterGray.png){width="800"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

::: {.border}
![](figs/mtCarsScatterSmall.png){width="800"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

::: {.border}
![](figs/mtCarsScatterGrid1.png){width="800"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

::: {.border}
![](figs/mtCarsScatterGrid2.png){width="800"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

::: {.border}
![](figs/mtCarsScatterGrid3.png){width="800"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

The white circles you see at the intersections is called the **"Hermann Grid illusion"**

::: {.border}
![](figs/mtCarsScatterGrid4.png){width="700"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

::: {.border}
![](figs/mtCarsScatterGrid5.png){width="800"}
:::

---

{{< inverse >}}
{{< center >}}

```{r}
#| echo: false

countdown(
  minutes = 5,
  warn_when = 30,
  update_every = 1,
  left = 0,
  right = 0,
  top = 1,
  bottom = 0,
  margin = "5%",
  font_size = "8em"
)
```

# Break!

## Stand up, Move around, Stretch!

---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis

agenda(3)
```

---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis

bp(0)
```

---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis

bp(1)
```

---

{{< inverse >}}
{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="20%"}
:::

::: {.col width="80%"}
# "_Erase non-data ink._"
## — Ed Tufte
:::

---

{{< center >}}
{{< middle >}}

::: {.col}
![](images/check-bad.png){width="80"}
![](images/monsterBars.png){width="500"}

Figure 1.6: `Monstrous Costs’ by Nigel Holmes, in Healy, 2018
:::

::: {.col .fragment}
![](images/check-good.png){width="100"}
![](figs/monsterBars.png){width="480"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

![](images/less-is-more-darkhorse-analytics.gif){width="800"}

[Figure 24.1: From Data Looks Better Naked by Darkhorse Analytics]{.left}

---

{{< center >}}
{{< middle >}}

::: {.col}
![](images/check-bad.png){width="80"}
![](images/less-is-more-darkhorse-analytics-before.png){width="500"}
:::

::: {.col .fragment}
![](images/check-good.png){width="100"}
![](images/less-is-more-darkhorse-analytics-after.png){width="480"}
:::

---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis

bp(2)
```

---

{{< center >}}
{{< middle >}}

::: {.col}
## Humans aren't good at distinguishing 3D space

![](images/penrose-stairs.png){width="400"}

[Penrose Stairs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Penrose_stairs), made famous by<br>M.C. Escher (1898-1972)
:::

::: {.col .fragment}
## Ink proportions !=<br>true proportions

![](images/3dpies.png){width="500"}
:::

---

{{< center >}}
{{< middle >}}

# Occlusion: geoms are obscured

![](images/3d-occlusion.png){width="400"}

---

{{< center >}}
{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="60%"}
![](images/3dbad.jpeg){width="100%"}
:::

::: {.col width="40%"}
# Multiple interpretations
:::

---

{{< center >}}
{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="40%"}
![](images/check-bad.png){width="80"}
![](images/3d-occlusion.png){width="400"}
:::

::: {.col width="60%" .fragment}
![](images/check-good.png){width="100"}

::: {.border}
![](figs/facetedBars.png){width="600"}
:::
:::

---

{{< center >}}
{{< middle >}}

# The third dimension distracts from the data

## (this is what Tufte calls "chart junk")

![](images/3d-chart-junk.png){width="700"}

---

{{< center >}}
{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="60%"}
![](images/check-bad.png){width="80"}
![](images/3d-chart-junk.png)
:::

::: {.col width="40%" .fragment}
![](images/check-good.png){width="100"}

::: {.border}
![](figs/simpleBars.png)
:::
:::

---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis

bp(3)
```

---

{{< center >}}
{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="60%" .border}
![](images/tufte-fuel-economy-bad.png)

[Edward Tufte (2001) "The Visual Display of Quantitative Information", 2nd Edition, pg. 57-58.]{.left .font80}
:::

::: {.col width="40%" .border .fragment}
![](images/tufte-fuel-economy-good.png){width="370"}
:::

---

{{< center >}}
{{< middle >}}

# Bar charts should always start at 0

::: {.col width="60%" .fragment}
![](images/check-bad.png){width="80"}
![](images/fox-news-bars.png)

[Image from http://livingqlikview.com/the-9-worst-data-visualizations-ever-created/]{.left .font70}
:::

::: {.col width="40%" .fragment}
![](images/check-good.png){width="100"}

::: {.border}
![](figs/barchart_zero.png){width="330"}
:::
:::

---

{{< center >}}
{{< middle >}}

# Don't cherry-pick your data

::: {.col width="60%" .fragment}
![](images/check-bad.png){width="80"}
![](images/fox-news-immigration-bars-bad.jpg)

[Image from https://www.mediamatters.org/fox-news/fox-news-newest-dishonest-chart-immigration-enforcement]{.left .font70}
:::

::: {.col width="40%" .fragment}
![](images/check-good.png){width="100"}

::: {.border}
![](images/fox-news-immigration-bars-good.jpg)
:::
:::

---

{{< center >}}
{{< middle >}}

# Make sure your chart makes sense

![](images/fox-news-piechart.png){width="600"}

[Image from Image from http://livingqlikview.com/the-9-worst-data-visualizations-ever-created/]{.left .font70}

---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis

bp(4)
```

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}
{{< bg-color "#ffffff" >}}

::: {.col width="45%"}
![](images/check-bad.png){width="80"}
![](figs/statesPie.png){width="750"}
:::

::: {.col width="55%" .fragment}
![](images/check-good.png){width="100"}
![](figs/statesBar.png){width="750"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< bg-color "#ffffff" >}}

::: {.col width="45%"}
# Exceptions:

## - Small data
## - Simple fractions
## - If sum of parts matters
:::

::: {.col width="55%" .fragment}
![](images/bundestag-pie-1.png)
:::

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}
{{< bg-color "#ffffff" >}}

# Best pie chart of all time

![](images/pie-pyramid.png){width="900"}

---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis

bp(5)
```

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

# Stacked bars are rarely a good idea

::: {.border}
![](figs/jr_diamonds_bars_stacked.png){width="700"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

## "Parallel coordinates" plot usually works better

::: {.col .border}
![](figs/jr_diamonds_line_cut.png)
:::

::: {.col .border}
![](figs/jr_diamonds_line_total.png)
:::

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

## Exception:<br>When you care about the _total_ more than the categories

::: {.col width="60%" .border}
![](figs/jr_phones_area.png)
:::

::: {.col width="40%" .border .fragment}
![](figs/backgroundChecks.png){width="400"}
:::

---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis

bp(6)
```

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

## Rotate axes if you can't read them

::: {.col width="45%"}
![](images/check-bad.png){width="80"}

::: {.border}
![](figs/mpg_bars_bad.png){width="400"}
:::
:::

::: {.col width="55%" .fragment}
![](images/check-good.png){width="100"}

::: {.border}
![](figs/mpg_bars_flipped.png){width="750"}
:::
:::

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

## Default order is almost always wrong

::: {.col}
![](images/check-bad.png){width="80"}

Ordered by alphabet (default)

::: {.border}
![](figs/mpg_bars_flipped.png)
:::
:::

::: {.col .fragment}
![](images/check-good.png){width="100"}

Ordered by count

::: {.border}
![](figs/mpg_bars_flipped_sorted.png)
:::
:::

---

{{< center >}}

## Exception: Ordinal variables

::: {.border}
![](images/wildlife-months-bar-1.png){width="900"}
:::

---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis

bp(7)
```

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

## Directly label geoms

::: {.col}
![](images/check-bad.png){width="75"}

::: {.border}
![](figs/milk_region.png)
:::
:::

::: {.col .fragment}
![](images/check-good.png){width="100"}

::: {.border}
![](figs/milk_region_label.png)
:::
:::

---

{{< center >}}

## Exception: When you have repeated categories

::: {.border}
![](images/pattern_fills2.png){width="600"}
:::

---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis

bp(8)
```

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}
{{< bg-color "#f8f8f8" >}}

![](images/pattern_fills3.png){width="600"}

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

::: {.col}
![](images/check-bad.png){width="75"}

::: {.border}
![](images/pattern_fills1.png)
:::
:::

::: {.col .fragment}
![](images/check-good.png){width="100"}

::: {.border}
![](images/pattern_fills2.png)
:::
:::

---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis

bp(9)
```

---

{{< center >}}

# 10% of males and 1% of females are color blind

::: {.col .fragment .border}
![](figs/colorBlindBad1.png)
:::

::: {.col .fragment .border}
![](figs/colorBlindBad2.png)
:::

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

## Facets can be used to avoid color altogether

::: {.col}
![](images/check-bad.png){width="75"}

::: {.border}
![](figs/colorBlindBad1.png)
:::
:::

::: {.col .fragment}
![](images/check-good.png){width="100"}

::: {.border}
![](figs/colorBlindBad3.png)
:::
:::

---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis

bp(10)
```

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< bg-color "#ffffff" >}}
{{< center >}}

![](images/pole-pie1.png){width="900"}

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< bg-color "#ffffff" >}}
{{< center >}}

![](images/pole-pie2.png){width="900"}

---

{{< center >}}
{{< middle >}}

## Who do you think did a better job in tonight’s debate?

| | Clinton | Trump |
|-|-|-|
| Among Democrats | 99% | 1% |
| Among Republicans | 53% | 47% |

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="20%"}
:::

::: {.col width="80%"}
# References:

# - [Data Viz "Cheat Sheet"](https://github.com/emse-eda-gwu/2026-Fall/raw/main/content/cheatsheet-dataviz.pdf)

# - [Data Viz Reference Page](https://eda.seas.gwu.edu/2026-Fall/references.html#visualizing-data)
:::

---

{{< inverse >}}

# Your turn - go [here](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1D3WMwY86lRe5W4VyCVejeSdf8esfcUQ4PnczzcmQ2eA/edit?usp=sharing)

For your "bad" visualization:

1) Identify where the graphic falls on Cleveland's pattern recognition hierarchy

![](images/cleveland-hierarchy.png){width="1000"}

2) Any design rules that are broken

3) Suggest at least two improvements

```{r}
#| echo: false

countdown(
  minutes = 10,
  warn_when = 30,
  update_every = 1,
  top = 0,
  right = 0,
  font_size = "2em"
)
```

---

{{< middle >}}
{{< center >}}

![](images/bad-viz/army_budget.jpg){width="900"}

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="40%"}
![](images/bad-viz/prisoners.png){width="500"}
:::

::: {.col width="60%" .fragment}
![](figs/prisoner_bars.png){width="900"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="40%"}
![](images/bad-viz/junk-life-expectancy-bad.png){width="500"}
:::

::: {.col width="60%" .fragment}
![](images/bad-viz/junk-life-expectancy-good1.png){width="400"}
![](images/bad-viz/junk-life-expectancy-good2.png){width="400"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col width="45%"}
![](images/bad-viz/bears-bad.png){width="500"}
:::

::: {.col width="55%" .fragment}
![](images/bad-viz/bears-good.jpeg){width="600"}
:::

---

{{< middle >}}

::: {.col}
![](images/bad-viz/clinton-tweet-venn-1.png)
:::

::: {.col .fragment}
![](images/bad-viz/clinton-tweet-venn-2.jpg)
:::

::: {.col .fragment}
![](figs/backgroundChecks.png)
:::

---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis

agenda(4)
```

---

{{< center >}}

## Making a (good) ggplot

::: {.col width="40%"}
Before:

![](figs/birds_before.png)
:::

::: {.col width="60%"}
After:

![](figs/birds_after.png)
:::

---

{{< inverse >}}

# [Making a (good) ggplot]{.center}

::: {.font120}
1. Format data frame
2. Add geoms
3. Flip coordinates?
4. Reorder factors?
5. Adjust scales
6. Adjust theme
7. Annotate
:::

---

# 1) Format data frame

```{r}
#| code-line-numbers: "3"

# Format the data frame
wildlife_impacts %>%
  count(operator)
```

---

# 2) Add geoms

::: {.col}
```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "8"

# Format the data frame
wildlife_impacts %>%
  count(operator) %>%

# Add geoms
  ggplot() +
  geom_col(
      aes(x = operator, y = n),
      width = 0.7, alpha = 0.8)
```
:::

::: {.col}
![](figs/birds_before.png)
:::

---

## 3) Flip coordinates - can you read the labels?

::: {.col}
::: {.font70}
```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "12"

# Format the data frame
wildlife_impacts %>%
  count(operator) %>%

# Add geoms
  ggplot() +
  geom_col(
      aes(x = operator, y = n),
      width = 0.7, alpha = 0.8) +

# Flip coordinates
  coord_flip()
```
:::
:::

::: {.col}
![](figs/birds_coord_flip.png){width="500"}
:::

---

## 3) Flip coordinates - can you read the labels?

::: {.col}
::: {.font70}
```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "8"

# Format the data frame
wildlife_impacts %>%
  count(operator) %>%

# Add geoms
  ggplot() +
  geom_col(
      aes(x = n, y = operator),
      width = 0.7, alpha = 0.8)
```
:::
:::

::: {.col}
![](figs/birds_coord_flip.png){width="500"}
:::

---

## 4) Reorder factors with `reorder()`

::: {.col width="55%"}
::: {.font70}
```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "8"

# Format the data frame
wildlife_impacts %>%
  count(operator) %>%

# Add geoms
  ggplot() +
  geom_col(
      aes(x = n, y = reorder(operator, n)),
      width = 0.7, alpha = 0.8)
```
:::
:::

::: {.col width="45%"}
![](figs/birds_fct_reorder.png)
:::

---

## 5) Adjust scales

::: {.col width="55%"}
::: {.font70}
```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "12,13"

# Format the data frame
wildlife_impacts %>%
  count(operator) %>%

# Add geoms
  ggplot() +
  geom_col(
      aes(x = n, y = reorder(operator, n)),
      width = 0.7, alpha = 0.8) +

# Adjust x axis scale
  scale_x_continuous(
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05)))
```
:::
:::

::: {.col width="45%"}
![](figs/birds_scales1.png)
:::

---

## 5) Adjust scales - customize break points (if you want)

::: {.col width="55%"}
::: {.font70}
```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "14,15"

# Format the data frame
wildlife_impacts %>%
  count(operator) %>%

# Add geoms
  ggplot() +
  geom_col(
      aes(x = n, y = reorder(operator, n)),
      width = 0.7, alpha = 0.8) +

# Adjust x axis scale
  scale_x_continuous(
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05)),
    breaks = c(0, 10000, 20000),
    limits = c(0, 20000))
```
:::
:::

::: {.col width="45%"}
![](figs/birds_scales2.png)
:::

---

## 6) Adjust theme

### Four `cowplot` themes you should know

![](figs/cowplot_themes.png){width="1200"}

---

## 6) Adjust theme

::: {.col}
For horizontal bars, add only vertical grid

::: {.font70}
```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "16"

# Format the data frame
wildlife_impacts %>%
  count(operator) %>%

# Add geoms
  ggplot() +
  geom_col(
      aes(x = n, y = reorder(operator, n)),
      width = 0.7, alpha = 0.8) +

# Adjust x axis scale
  scale_x_continuous(
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +

# Adjust theme
  theme_minimal_vgrid()
```
:::
:::

::: {.col}
![](figs/birds_theme.png){width="500"}
:::

---

## 7) Annotate

::: {.col}
::: {.font70}
```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "4,20,21,22"

# Format the data frame
wildlife_impacts %>%
  count(operator) %>%
  mutate(operator = str_to_title(operator)) %>%

# Add geoms
  ggplot() +
  geom_col(
      aes(x = n, y = reorder(operator, n)),
      width = 0.7, alpha = 0.8) +

# Adjust x axis scale
  scale_x_continuous(
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +

# Adjust theme
  theme_minimal_vgrid() +

# Annotate
  labs(
    x = 'Count',
    y = NULL)
```
:::
:::

::: {.col}
![](figs/birds_after.png)
:::

---

## Finished product

::: {.col width="55%"}
::: {.font70}
```{r}
#| eval: false

wildlife_impacts %>%
  count(operator) %>%
  mutate(operator = str_to_title(operator)) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_col(
      aes(x = n, y = reorder(operator, n)),
      width = 0.7, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  theme_minimal_vgrid() +
  labs(
    x = 'Count',
    y = NULL)
```
:::
:::

::: {.col width="45%"}
![](figs/birds_after.png)
:::

---

{{< inverse >}}

```{r}
#| echo: false

countdown(
  minutes = 15,
  warn_when = 30,
  update_every = 1,
  top = 0,
  right = 0,
  font_size = "2em"
)
```

## Your turn

::: {.col width="35%"}
Use the `gapminder.csv` data to create the following plot, following these steps:

1. Format data frame
2. Add geoms
3. Flip coordinates?
4. Reorder factors?
5. Adjust scales
6. Adjust theme
7. Annotate
:::

::: {.col width="65%"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 7

gapminder %>%
  filter(year == 2007, continent == "Americas") %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_col(aes(x = lifeExp, y = reorder(country, lifeExp)),
           width = 0.7, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  theme_minimal_vgrid(font_size = 18) +
  labs(
    title = 'Life expectancy in 2007 for\nCountries in the Americas',
    x = 'Life expectancy (years)',
    y = 'Country')
```
:::
